architecture.summary: ASAp is a decoding algorithm that operates on top of existing grammar-constrained decoding (GCD) systems. It iteratively samples from an LLM while enforcing grammar constraints. The key mechanism is maintaining an overapproximation of expected future grammaticality (EFG)—the probability that a continuation of a prefix will land in the grammar—by tracking all previously sampled sequences. After each sample is generated, the algorithm backpropagates through the prefix tree to refine EFG estimates for all prefixes, using observed token likelihoods to narrow the overapproximation. Sampling at each step uses the refined EFG estimates to reweight the LLM's token probabilities, balancing grammar adherence with distribution fidelity.

claims.statement: existing grammar-constrained decoding (GCD) techniques can distort the LLM's distribution, leading to outputs that are grammatical but appear with likelihoods that are not proportional to the ones given by the LLM
ASAp guarantees the output to be grammatical while provably producing outputs that match the conditional probability of the LLM's distribution conditioned on the given grammar constraint
the overapproximation ̂EFG_t is an upper bound on the expected future grammaticality EFG (Theorem 1)

as the number of iterations approaches infinity, the overapproximation ̂EFG_t converges to the exact expected future grammaticality EFG, and thus ASAp converges to exact samples from the constrained LLM distribution (Theorem 2)
on SLIA benchmarks, sum of least square difference between expectations computed by ASAp and target expectations is 0.087, compared to GCD's 0.169
on INV-BV4 benchmarks, sum of least square difference between expectations computed by ASAp and target expectations is 0.149, compared to GCD's 0.392

ASAp produces correct samples 38% more often than GCD (geomean) on INV-BV benchmarks; however on SLIA benchmarks that both tools can solve, ASAp produces correct samples 73% less often than GCD (geomean)
the key limitation of this work is the current slow convergence of the ASAp algorithm; on some benchmarks, even after 2,000 iterations the KL divergence barely improves
